verses.text: Pharaoh's cupbearer and baker have offended him. He throws them in prison — the same prison where Joseph is being held. The two highest officials of the palace find themselves where the falsely-accused slave already is. God places witnesses inside Joseph's cell.

Pharaoh's cupbearer and baker have offended him. He throws them in prison — the same prison where Joseph is being held. The two highest officials of the palace find themselves where the falsely-accused slave already is. God places witnesses inside Joseph's cell.

Pharaoh's cupbearer and baker have offended him. He throws them in prison — the same prison where Joseph is being held. The two highest officials of the palace find themselves where the falsely-accused slave already is. God places witnesses inside Joseph's cell.

The captain of the guard assigns Joseph to serve them. They are there for some time. The man who once managed Potiphar's household now waits on prisoners. The promotions keep happening, even underground.
One night, both men dream — each his own dream, each with its own meaning. Two dreams in one night, in a foreign prison. The text wants us to notice God arranging the moment.

Joseph comes to them in the morning and sees their faces are troubled. He asks — why do you look so sad today? In the middle of his own pain, he is still paying attention to other people's faces. That's the kind of man God uses.

Joseph comes to them in the morning and sees their faces are troubled. He asks — why do you look so sad today? In the middle of his own pain, he is still paying attention to other people's faces. That's the kind of man God uses.
We dreamed dreams, they say, and there is no interpreter. Joseph: do interpretations not belong to God? Tell me, please. He gives God the credit for the gift before he has even used it.

The cupbearer tells his dream: a vine with three branches, budding, blossoming, ripening into grapes. I took the grapes, pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and gave it into his hand. A picture of the work he used to do.

The cupbearer tells his dream: a vine with three branches, budding, blossoming, ripening into grapes. I took the grapes, pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and gave it into his hand. A picture of the work he used to do.

The cupbearer tells his dream: a vine with three branches, budding, blossoming, ripening into grapes. I took the grapes, pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and gave it into his hand. A picture of the work he used to do.
Joseph answers without hesitation: the three branches are three days. In three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your office. You will hand Pharaoh his cup again, as before.
Joseph answers without hesitation: the three branches are three days. In three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your office. You will hand Pharaoh his cup again, as before.

But remember me when it goes well with you. Show me kindness. Mention me to Pharaoh and get me out of this house. I was stolen out of the land of the Hebrews; I have done nothing to deserve this dungeon. Joseph speaks the truth about his life out loud — perhaps for the first time.

But remember me when it goes well with you. Show me kindness. Mention me to Pharaoh and get me out of this house. I was stolen out of the land of the Hebrews; I have done nothing to deserve this dungeon. Joseph speaks the truth about his life out loud — perhaps for the first time.

Seeing that the interpretation was good, the chief baker tells his dream too. Three white baskets on my head; in the top one, baked goods for Pharaoh. But birds were eating them out of the basket on my head. He hopes for the same answer.

Seeing that the interpretation was good, the chief baker tells his dream too. Three white baskets on my head; in the top one, baked goods for Pharaoh. But birds were eating them out of the basket on my head. He hopes for the same answer.

Joseph answers gently but plainly: the three baskets are three days. In three days Pharaoh will lift up your head — but from you, and hang you on a tree, and birds will eat your flesh. Truth from God isn't always good news for everyone.

Joseph answers gently but plainly: the three baskets are three days. In three days Pharaoh will lift up your head — but from you, and hang you on a tree, and birds will eat your flesh. Truth from God isn't always good news for everyone.

Three days later, on Pharaoh's birthday, he makes a feast and lifts up both heads — the cupbearer's, the baker's, in front of all his servants. The same phrase, two different fates. Even the wording was prophetic.
The cupbearer is restored. He hands Pharaoh his cup again, exactly as Joseph predicted. The baker is hanged. Joseph's word held in both directions — comfort and judgment.
The cupbearer is restored. He hands Pharaoh his cup again, exactly as Joseph predicted. The baker is hanged. Joseph's word held in both directions — comfort and judgment.

But the cupbearer did not remember Joseph. He forgot him. Joseph would wait two more years in prison before that one forgetful man finally remembered. God's promises don't always arrive on the schedule we asked for.
